1: The psychological collapse of all the Canaanite kings upon hearing of the Jordan crossing — 'their hearts melted and there was no longer any spirit in them' — types the response of every power and principality to the resurrection of Christ. 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). The Canaanite kings were not merely militarily discouraged; they were theologically overwhelmed — their divine system had no answer to YHWH's Jordan miracle. The powers of darkness had no answer to Christ's resurrection — their ultimate weapon (death) had been defeated. The 'melting heart' of the Canaanite kings types the 'open shame' of the disarmed principalities after the cross.
2: The circumcision at Gilgal with flint knives types Christ's 'circumcision made without hands' — the death of the body of flesh in which he bore all sin. 'In him also you were circumcised with a circumcision made without hands, by putting off the body of the flesh, by the circumcision of Christ, having been buried with him in baptism' (Colossians 2:11-12). The Gilgal circumcision was the covenant-entry rite before the conquest battles; the circumcision of Christ (his death) is the covenant-entry event that opens the new creation. The flint knife cutting away the foreskin types the cross cutting away the 'body of flesh' — the sin-dominated human nature — that Christ bore in his death.
3: The 'Hill of the Foreskins' — Gibeath-haaraloth — became a named place precisely because of the covenant sign enacted there. The cross has the same naming power: 'Golgotha, which means Place of a Skull' (Matthew 27:33) — the place where the ultimate circumcision occurred became permanently named for the event. As Gibeath-haaraloth was the site of the covenant sign that prepared Israel for conquest, Golgotha was the site of the covenant act that accomplished the ultimate conquest. Both are 'named hills of the covenant sign' — hills where the flesh was cut away so that the people could enter their inheritance.
4: The explanation of why the wilderness generation was uncircumcised — all who came from Egypt had died under judgment — types the condition of humanity before Christ: all who came from Adam's Egypt were under death's sentence and bore the uncircumcised flesh of the sin-nature. 'For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God' (Romans 3:23). The wilderness generation's uncircumcised state was not accidental but the result of inherited judgment. Christ comes as the new Joshua to the generation born in the wilderness — those who have never been able to produce the righteousness the law demands — and performs the circumcision of the cross that the law-period could never accomplish.
5: The distinction between those who came out of Egypt (circumcised, under the old covenant) and those born in the wilderness (uncircumcised, needing the new covenant rite) types the transition from old to new covenant. The circumcised generation died in the wilderness — the old covenant generation did not enter the rest (Hebrews 3:17-19). The uncircumcised wilderness generation entered the land through the new Gilgal rite — the new covenant community enters the inheritance not through the old covenant circumcision but through the new. 'For in Christ Jesus neither circumcision nor uncircumcision counts for anything, but a new creation' (Galatians 6:15). Gilgal's mass circumcision is simultaneously the end of the wilderness period and the beginning of the new creation.
6: 'Forty years the people of Israel walked in the wilderness, until all the nation, the men of war who came out of Egypt, perished, because they did not obey the voice of the LORD' — the forty-year judgment types the period of the law's administration (Galatians 3:19-25: 'the law was our guardian until Christ came'). The law kept Israel in the wilderness of inability; it could diagnose sin but not cure it. 'Now before faith came, we were held captive under the law, imprisoned until the coming faith would be revealed' (Galatians 3:23). The disobedient generation that perished in the wilderness types those who trust in the law for righteousness and find only death; the Gilgal generation types those who receive the new covenant circumcision through faith in Christ.
7: 'Their children, whom he raised up in their place, Joshua circumcised' — the raising up of children in place of the dead generation types the resurrection itself. 'For if we have been united with him in a death like his, we shall certainly be united with him in a resurrection like his' (Romans 6:5). The dead wilderness generation is replaced by the Gilgal generation as the old humanity is replaced by the new: 'The first man Adam became a living being; the last Adam became a life-giving spirit' (1 Corinthians 15:45). Christ as the last Adam is the ultimate 'child raised up in place of the dead' — the new humanity who succeeds where the first failed, receiving the inheritance that Adam forfeited.
8: The healing period after mass circumcision — the entire army vulnerable, waiting in camp — types the three days of Christ's death in which he was apparently vulnerable and defeated. On Good Friday and Holy Saturday, Christ lay in the tomb — the church appeared defenseless, the disciples scattered, the disciples' hopes destroyed. The Gilgal camp during healing was similarly: if Jericho's army had attacked in those days, Israel would have been destroyed. But YHWH's protection covered them — as the Father's purposes covered the three days of Christ's apparent vulnerability in the tomb. 'For you will not abandon my soul to Hades, or let your Holy One see corruption' (Acts 2:27, citing Psalms 16:10). The healing period preceded the conquest's first victory.
9: 'Today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt from you' — the Gilgal etymology (galal, to roll) types the atonement's removal of sin's reproach. 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). The 'reproach of Egypt' — the shame of slavery, uncircumcision, and divine judgment — was rolled away at Gilgal as the cross rolls away the reproach of sin, death, and divine condemnation. 'There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus' (Romans 8:1) — the Egypt-reproach is gone; the Gilgal-moment of the cross has occurred. The naming of Gilgal ('rolling') is YHWH's memorial of the atonement's shame-removing work, fulfilled in Christ who 'endured the cross, despising the shame' (Hebrews 12:2).
10: The first Passover in the land — on the fourteenth of Nisan at Gilgal — completes the liturgical arc that began with the first Passover in Egypt (Exod 12). The NT's Passover completion is Christ's last supper and death: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed' (1 Corinthians 5:7). Jesus died on Passover, the fourteenth of Nisan, the same calendar date as the Gilgal Passover — the type and anti-type sharing the same date. The Gilgal Passover was celebrated in the land at the beginning of the conquest; the cross-Passover was accomplished at the beginning of the new creation's conquest over sin and death. Both are 'in the land' Passovers — eaten by those who have crossed through the waters and arrived.
11: The eating of the produce of the land on the day after the Passover — the first taste of Canaanite grain — types the new spiritual nourishment available in Christ. Jesus draws precisely this typological line: 'Your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, and they died... I am the living bread that came down from heaven. If anyone eats of this bread, he will live forever' (John 6:49-51). The transition from manna (miraculous wilderness food) to land produce (ordinary grain made extraordinary by covenant context) types the transition from the provisional sustenance of the law period to the full nourishment of Christ. The Eucharistic bread is this 'produce of the land' — ordinary bread made the body of Christ by covenant institution.
12: 'And the manna ceased the day after they ate of the produce of the land' — the cessation of manna is the definitive ending of the wilderness provision system. 'For the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ' (John 1:17). The manna types the law — essential and life-sustaining for the wilderness period, but designed to cease when the land was entered. John 6 makes the typological argument explicit: 'Your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, and they died.' The manna was good but not ultimate; it sustained life temporarily but could not conquer death. Christ is the bread that ends the manna-era: 'The one who eats this bread will live forever' (John 6:58). The manna's cessation is not loss but fulfillment — the land's produce has arrived.
13: The appearance of the man with the drawn sword — encountered by Joshua alone, near Jericho, after the Passover — is the narrative's most explicit Christophany. Joshua 'fell on his face to the earth and worshiped' (Joshua 5:14) — worship, not merely greeting. The command to remove sandals (Joshua 5:15) identifies the figure's presence as the same divine presence who spoke to Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:5). The figure is the pre-incarnate Christ, the Angel of YHWH who appears as a warrior throughout the OT: 'He who appeared to Abraham at Mamre, to Jacob at Jabbok, to Moses at Sinai' — the same figure who will be incarnated as Jesus of Nazareth. The drawn sword types both the word of God (Hebrews 4:12, 'the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword') and Christ as the divine warrior whose wrath and grace are both in his hand.
14: 'No; but I am the commander of the army of the LORD. Now I have come' — the Christophany's self-identification is military: the divine warrior taking personal command of the conquest. The NT equivalent is the risen Christ in Revelation 19:11-16: 'Then I saw heaven opened, and behold, a white horse! The one sitting on it is called Faithful and True, and in righteousness he judges and makes war... His name is called the Word of God. And the armies of heaven... were following him on white horses.' The Commander of YHWH's army at Gilgal is the same figure as the Rider on the white horse — the divine warrior who takes command at the beginning of each conquest phase. Joshua's response — prostration and 'what does my lord say to his servant?' — is the only appropriate response to this Commander.
15: 'Take off your sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy' — the command that repeats Exodus 3:5 word for word identifies the pre-incarnate Christ with the one who spoke to Moses at the burning bush. The same presence, the same ground-holiness, the same sandal-removal command: YHWH's commissioning presence before Moses at Sinai and before Joshua at Gilgal is the same pre-incarnate Word who will be incarnated as Jesus. 'Before Abraham was, I am' (John 8:58) — the eternal Word has been appearing and commissioning throughout Israel's history. The holy ground at Gilgal is holy because the incarnate Word who will stand on a hill outside Jerusalem is already present there, commissioning the conquest that is a shadow of the ultimate conquest he will accomplish in his own body.
